items.title: Merge vs rebase — same content, different history
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB
  subgraph Before["Before — feature diverged at D"]
    D1((D)) --> E1((E)) --> F1((F))
    D1 --> A1((A)) --> B1((B)) --> C1((C))
  end
  subgraph Merge["After git merge (true merge, two parents on M)"]
    D2((D)) --> E2((E)) --> F2((F)) --> M((M))
    D2 --> A2((A)) --> B2((B)) --> C2((C)) --> M
  end
  subgraph Rebase["After git rebase main (linear, new SHAs)"]
    D3((D)) --> E3((E)) --> F3((F)) --> Ap((A')) --> Bp((B')) --> Cp((C'))
  end
items.caption: Merge keeps both parents and shows the divergence; rebase rewrites the feature commits to sit on top, producing a single line.

items.title: Fast-forward vs --no-ff merge
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  subgraph FF["Fast-forward (main hadn't moved)"]
    D1((D)) --> E1((E)) --> A1((A)) --> B1((B))
  end
  subgraph NoFF["--no-ff (always make a merge commit)"]
    D2((D)) --> E2((E)) --> M2((M))
    D2 --> A2((A)) --> B2((B)) --> M2
  end
items.caption: Fast-forward leaves no trace of the branch; `--no-ff` always creates a merge commit so the branch is visible in history.
